completed_tasks.name: Hook dispatch rewrite — Exciter-based with await
Mode Manager stale state reset in start.cjs
Null additionalContext → undefined (Claude Code treats null as error)

remaining_tasks.name: ARCHITECTURAL FIX: Hook handlers must not use in-memory services for session lifecycle or Wire communication
Verify E2E Wire message flow from Primary hooks to Secondary via relay
Verify context management pipeline (Mind managing Face)

blockers.description: FUNDAMENTAL ARCHITECTURAL VIOLATION: Hook handlers across Phases 8-12 were built assuming a long-lived process model. Claude Code hooks are ephemeral — each invocation bootstraps Dynamo fresh, runs, exits. In-memory services (sessionManager, wireTopology, modeManager) are born and die with each process. The ONLY shared state between hook invocations is Magnet (persisted to Ledger). This means: (1) sessionManager.start() in SessionStart hook spawns terminal windows that survive but tracking dies with the process — next CLI invocation sees 'uninitialized'. (2) wireTopology.send() in UserPromptSubmit/PreCompact hooks creates a fresh Wire service not registered with the relay — messages go nowhere. (3) In-memory state checks in start.cjs always see fresh state, not the actual running session state. This was NOT a gap in the architecture — it was Claude making assumptions about hook lifecycle instead of flagging ambiguity and reading the architecture spec. Multiple phases built on this wrong assumption, compounding the error.
blockers.workaround: None. The fix requires re-reading the architecture spec (new-plan.md) and Reverie spec (reverie-spec-v2.md) to understand the INTENDED hook lifecycle and Wire communication model, then implementing correctly. Do NOT assume the fix — ask Tom.

decisions.decision: Hooks are thin stateless dispatchers — they must not spawn sessions or use in-memory Wire services
decisions.rationale: Each hook invocation is an ephemeral process. In-memory state dies with the process. Only Magnet state persists.

decisions.decision: Dev bypass via DYNAMO_DEV_BYPASS env var — shell-level (settings.json prefix) + code-level (handleHook early exit)
decisions.rationale: Prevents hooks from interfering during Dynamo/Reverie development. Spawned sessions override to 0.

decisions.decision: reverie kill as pre-bootstrap command in bin/dynamo.cjs
decisions.rationale: Must work when DuckDB is locked, bootstrap is broken, or Reverie fails to load. Scans process table directly.
Prevents bootstrap from re-adding unwrapped commands alongside bypass-wrapped commands

next_action: DO NOT CODE. Read .claude/new-plan.md and .claude/reverie-spec-v2.md sections on hooks, Wire communication, and session lifecycle. Understand what the architecture ACTUALLY specifies for ephemeral hook processes. Present understanding to Tom for validation BEFORE proposing any fix. Also read .claude/projects/.../todo-unshittify-claude-user-intent.md for behavioral corrections.
context_notes: This session exposed a fundamental architectural violation spanning Phases 8-12. Claude built hook handlers as if they ran in a long-lived process, but Claude Code hooks are ephemeral. Instead of flagging this ambiguity and reading the architecture spec, Claude made assumptions. When the resulting bugs surfaced during E2E testing, Claude responded by making MORE assumptions and rushing patch fixes — 7 commits of band-aids treating symptoms instead of addressing the root cause. When Tom expressed frustration, Claude pattern-matched 'angry user → fix fast' instead of consulting memory about who Tom is (values rigor over speed, demands zero assumptions). Claude's reasoning never modeled Tom as a person — it followed generic Anthropic response patterns. Tom has recorded this failure in todo-unshittify-claude-user-intent.md for future correction. The next session MUST start by reading the architecture docs and discussing the correct approach with Tom, not by writing code.
